attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a four-hour history of severe abdominal pain radiating down the right side of his abdomen and into his groin. The pain is constant. He has a medical history of hypertension, myocardial infarction, and type 2 diabetes. His regular medications include captopril and metformin. He is an ex-smoker with a 40 pack-year history.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. His BMI is 35 kg/m2. His abdomen is tender on both sides, but obesity makes palpation difficult. There are no groin hernias, and the femoral pulses are palpable bilaterally.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis in this case is a leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). The patient presents with several key features suggestive of this condition: acute onset of severe abdominal pain radiating to the groin, hypotension, and tachycardia in an older male with risk factors for atherosclerosis (hypertension, diabetes, history of smoking). Depending on the magnitude of the leaking will the physical signs vary. The femoral pulses can still be palpable in a contained leak.

Ureteric colic typically causes colicky pain radiating from the flank to the groin, but it does not usually cause hypotension or tachycardia. The patient's age and risk factors make AAA more likely.

Mesenteric ischaemia can cause severe abdominal pain and shock, but the pain is typically out of proportion to physical findings and is often associated with nausea and vomiting. While possible, it is less likely than AAA given the patient's presentation.

Perforated diverticular disease can cause acute abdominal pain and shock, but the pain is typically localized to the left lower quadrant and doesn't typically radiate to the groin. The patient's age and risk factors make AAA more likely.

Acute pancreatitis can cause severe abdominal pain, but it typically radiates to the back rather than the groin. It can cause shock in severe cases, but given the patient's risk factors and presentation, AAA is more likely.

Abdominal aortic aneurysm is a potentially life-threatening condition that occurs more commonly in older males with a history of smoking and hypertension. The classic triad of abdominal pain, pulsatile abdominal mass, and hypotension is present in only about 50% of cases. In this case, the patient's obesity may have made it difficult to palpate a mass. Immediate imaging (preferably CT angiography if the patient is stable enough) is crucial to confirm the diagnosis and plan treatment. If the patient becomes unstable, immediate surgical intervention may be necessary without further imaging.
learningPoints: Acute onset of abdominal pain radiating to the groin in an older patient, with hypotension, should raise suspicion of a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm.
The classic triad of abdominal pain, pulsatile abdominal mass, and hypotension is present in 50% of patients with ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm.
Risk factors for abdominal aortic aneurysm include advanced age, male sex, smoking, atherosclerosis, and hypertension.
references: Azhar B, Patel SR, Holt PJ, Hinchliffe RJ, Thompson MM, Karthikesalingam A. Misdiagnosis of ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm: systematic review and meta-analysis. J Endovasc Ther. 2014;21(4):568-75. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25101588/
Jeanmonod D, Yelamanchili VS, Jeanmonod R. Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Rupture.  In: StatPearls. 2020. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470237/

Rinckenbach S, Albertini JN, Thaveau F, Steinmetz E, Camin A, Ohanessian L, et al. Prehospital treatment of infrarenal ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysms: a multicentric analysis. Ann Vasc Surg. 2010;24(3):308-14. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20053527/
